title: The button that is not a button
checkLabels.t3: The button knows what it does
why.no-button: There is no real button. There is a group labelled "button", which is something else entirely. The tag is <button>.
why.fake-button: The fake button is still there. Delete it — one button is enough, as long as it is real.
why.no-type: The button is there, but it does not say what it does. Without type="submit" the form may not submit at all — browsers differ.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: Look at the form's button. Run it. It looks like a button. Add CSS and it will be indistinguishable from a real one.
blocks.1.text: Except it is not. It is a <div> labelled "button" — a group that means nothing.
blocks.3.text: Put the mouse away. Press Tab a few times — the cursor moves through the fields.
blocks.4.text: And then? It stops. The button is never reached. Never.
blocks.5.text: Try Enter. Nothing. Try Space. Nothing.
blocks.6.text: Your form is fillable but unsendable — for anyone who does not use a mouse.
blocks.9.text: Change it and try again. Tab reaches it. Enter presses it. So does Space. A focus ring appears around it, so you can see where you are.
blocks.10.text: All of that is free. You wrote not one line for it.
blocks.12.text: type="submit" — submits the form. It is the default inside a <form>, but write it explicitly.
blocks.13.text: type="reset" — clears everything. Do not use it. Someone will hit it by mistake instead of "send" and lose everything they wrote.
blocks.14.text: type="button" — does nothing on its own. You attach JavaScript to it later.
blocks.15.text: Why so many people get this wrong
blocks.16.text: Because a <div> is easier to style. A real button comes with built-in styles you have to strip away.
blocks.17.text: So: people build a fake button to save themselves three lines of CSS. And they pay by making the button useless to anyone without a mouse.
blocks.18.text: Three lines of CSS against all the functionality. A bad trade.
blocks.19.text: If you have to add code to make something behave like a button — it should have been a button.
blocks.21.text: The reverse mistake is just as common: an <a> that leads nowhere but does something.
blocks.22.text: The rule is simple. Does it lead somewhere — a link. Does it do something — a button.
blocks.23.text: Your turn. Make the button real.
